Research Blog

Understanding Transformer Attention Mechanisms

Attention mechanisms are the core innovation behind the transformer architecture. They allow models to weigh the importance of different tokens when producing an output, enabling long-range dependencies.

The self-attention layer computes queries, keys, and values from the input embeddings. Each token attends to every other token in the sequence, producing a weighted sum of values based on query-key similarity.

Multi-head attention extends this by running several attention operations in parallel, each with different learned projections. This lets the model attend to different types of relationships simultaneously.

Cross-attention, used in encoder-decoder architectures, allows the decoder to attend to encoder outputs while generating predictions. This is fundamental to sequence-to-sequence tasks like translation.

Recent efficiency improvements, including sparse attention and linear attention variants, reduce the quadratic complexity of standard attention. These techniques make transformers practical for very long sequences.

Posted in Research
